caption.text: What to put on your landing page when you don't have social proof yet?
Well that's a really good question
I literally got the microphone out to answer it.
It's true that when you're starting out,
you rarely have social proof to highlight.
and that's completely normal.
First thing to know is that social proof
is a section that gathers testimonials,
key figures, or even logos of companies that have already trusted you.
So in itself, it's a reassurance section.
And the real problem isn't the absence of social proof or testimonials,
but the absence of elements that build trust.
So to reassure, you don't necessarily need to lie.
And personally, I'm not for it.
But you do need to replace classic
social proof with other forms of trust.
For example, it could be by showing the product in action.
not just a nice mockup, but the product performing a key action.
Because the more the user sees how it works
the less they need to be told that it works.
It can also be by showing the product's intelligence. how it reasons,
how it prioritizes an action,
how it avoids an error,
what technology is used.
When the user sees that the product understands what they are doing,
they immediately trust it.
Then it can be by reducing risks.
For example, you can include mentions like free trial,
no credit card required, no commitment,
even easy cancellation. because when the risk is low,
social proof becomes secondary.
Here, I've only listed a few examples to adapt to your product,
but there are
plenty of ways to reassure the user without needing social proof.
And it will depend on many factors like your product,
your target audience, what you offer. But as long as you are transparent,
that you clarify and reduce the risk,
your landing page can convert even without social proof.
